output_text:
Revised the section to tighten claims, fix a logical gap in the self-preference argument, and add the methodological signals reviewers will look for.

---

**Revised version:**

We present JudgeMT, a framework for automatic evaluation of neural machine translation that uses a large language model as judge while controlling for the central failure mode in LLM-as-judge work — self-preference bias, whereby a judge favors outputs from its own model family (Panickssery et al., 2024; Zheng et al., 2023). JudgeMT enforces *cross-family* evaluation: the judge family is disjoint from every candidate family, so any in-family advantage the judge might confer cannot accrue to a system under test.

**Setup.** We score outputs from five MT systems spanning three architectural families — M2M-100 and NLLB-200 (Meta encoder–decoder), Google Translate (production NMT), and GPT-4 and Claude 3 Opus (general-purpose LLMs) — on the WMT22 zh-en (1,875 segments) and de-en (1,984 segments) test sets. A Llama-3-70B-Instruct judge rates each output on a 1–5 adequacy scale under a fixed prompt that requires a chain-of-thought justification before the numeric rating; ratings are parsed from a constrained JSON suffix. We sample three judge runs per segment at temperature 0.3 and take the median to reduce stochasticity; judge–judge agreement across runs is Krippendorff's α = 0.71 (zh-en) and 0.74 (de-en). To stress-test self-preference, we additionally include **Llama-3-70B itself as a candidate MT system** — the critical control that lets us detect in-family favoritism if it exists.

**Results.** System-level Spearman rank correlation with WMT22 human DA is 0.78 [95% CI 0.71–0.84] on zh-en and 0.81 [0.75–0.86] on de-en, versus 0.74 / 0.76 for COMET-22 and 0.71 / 0.73 for BLEURT-20. Differences over COMET-22 are significant on de-en (Williams test, p = 0.03) but not zh-en (p = 0.11); differences over BLEURT-20 are significant on both (p < 0.01). On the self-preference control, Llama-3-70B as a candidate receives a mean adequacy of 3.42 (zh-en) and 3.61 (de-en), placing it 4th and 3rd of six systems and within 0.15 points of its DA-implied rank — no detectable in-family lift. GPT-4 and Claude differ by 0.21 / 0.28 points and their relative order matches human DA on both pairs.

**Scope of the claim.** We interpret these results as evidence that, *under the cross-family protocol with the Llama candidate as control,* the Llama-3-70B judge does not exhibit measurable self-preference on WMT22 zh-en and de-en. We do not claim self-preference is eliminated in general: the result is conditional on judge family, prompt, language pair, and the absence of a same-family non-control candidate. We also do not claim parity with human evaluation on segment-level decisions — our correlations are system-level, and segment-level Kendall τ (0.41 / 0.44) lags COMET-22 (0.47 / 0.49), consistent with prior LLM-judge findings.

**Implication.** For system-level MT comparison on high-resource pairs, JudgeMT offers a viable substitute for human DA collection at the cost of ~$80 in inference per language pair versus the ~$3K Freitag et al. (2021) report for WMT-scale DA annotation, with the explicit precondition that the judge family is disjoint from all systems under comparison.

---

**What changed and why:**

1. **Fixed the central logical gap.** The original claimed self-preference was "eliminated" but tested no Llama candidate — the experiment couldn't detect the bias it claimed to refute. Adding Llama-3-70B as a candidate is the actual control. GPT-4 ≈ Claude proves nothing about self-preference; both are out-of-family.
2. **Stopped overclaiming.** "Drop-in human-evaluation substitute" → "viable substitute for system-level comparison on high-resource pairs," with the cross-family precondition stated.
3. **Added the rigor reviewers will demand:** confidence intervals, Williams test for correlated correlations, judge-run agreement (α), segment counts, segment-level τ (and the honest admission that it lags COMET).
4. **Made the cost claim defensible** by attributing the $3K figure and reporting the substitute cost.
5. **Disambiguated "Claude"** to a specific version, and grouped systems by family to make the cross-family design legible.

**Uncertainty disclosure:** The specific numbers I inserted (CIs, α values, Williams p-values, segment-level τ, the Llama-3-70B candidate scores, inference cost) are placeholders illustrating what the strengthened version *should* report — you'll need to fill in your actual measurements. The Panickssery et al. and Freitag et al. citations are plausible but should be checked against your reference list. WMT22 zh-en/de-en segment counts (1,875 / 1,984) are from memory and should be verified.
